glossary.definition: Mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows. However it is even more challenging to measure animal intelligence than human intelligence, because animal diversity is even greater taking into account their cognitive and perceptual capacities, available sensory organs and the ways how they explore the environment in which they live.

glossary.definition: Not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) . glossary.sentence: Knowledge about plant intelligence is even more limited.

glossary.definition: Underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system. Therefore, research works focus on how information is represented, processed, transformed, and stored in the human mind and in computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4). Each category of definitions of artificial intelligence is based on a specific approach used to develop intelligent computer systems (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the categories use two standards of intelligent performance (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the categories on the left include definitions that adopt human intelligence as a standard; the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: Underlying systems that act humanly and is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence. It has the following idea. There are 3 rooms. In the first one there is an artificial intelligence, in the second room - a human being or natural intelligence, and in the third room there is a tester (Fig.5). The tester does not see nor artificial intelligence, nor human being and asks questions to both intelligences using a text-based input tool. If the tester cannot differentiate which answers were given by the human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence, than the artificial intelligence is intelligent.

glossary.sentence: It assumes the development of such intelligent computer systems that can reach human-level performance in all cognitive tasks.

A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: Maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does.

BioScience , 66(7), pp. DOI 10.1093/ biosci /biw048 Topic 1.2 - Artificial Intelligence - a Sub-Field of Computer Science Artificial intelligence inherited many of ideas, viewpoints, and techniques from other scientific fields. Its roots lay in the following scientific fields (Russell and Norvig, 2020) : Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge. This knowledge is encoded using some internal language of the device and can be used to find a correct action. Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory. Mathematicians provided tools to work with logical statements and with uncertain, probabilistic judgments. They also laid the foundations for reasoning algorithms. Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents. Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information. This led to emergence of artificial neural networks which simulate neurons of the human brain and can be used for classification, pattern recognition, optimization, management, and other tasks. Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines. The man research questions are related to how humans and animals think and act. Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications. Artificial intelligence applications tend to be complex and large, and they could not work without the progress in computer speed and memory capacity, provided by the computer industry. Thus, in this field of science the basic question is how to develop efficient computers. Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer. The main research question is how intelligent artefacts can operate under their own control. Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines. Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: Describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales. Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment . Brooks , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment. The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours
glossary.sentence: The main idea is that intelligence is a capability that arises when agents with simpler behaviours interact in the real environment and together achieve more complex behaviours

glossary.definition: is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.

glossary.sentence: There are a large number of rules that match the data of the problem and thus produce an increasing number of conclusions or goals.

glossary.definition: Outcomes produced by rules that match the data of the problem.
glossary.sentence: There are a large number of rules that match the data of the problem and thus produce an increasing number of conclusions or goals.

glossary.definition: Outcomes produced by rules that match the data of the problem.
glossary.sentence: There are a large number of rules that match the data of the problem and thus produce an increasing number of conclusions or goals.

glossary.definition: The direction in which the search algorithm explores the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: To select a search direction, it is necessary to analyse a problem by taking the following factors into account: availability of data about the problem; easiness of defining a search goal; the structure of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: Nodes that can be reached directly from a given node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: A closed loop in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node. Thus it would be logical to prevent the search algorithm from entering cycles, even though they are allowed in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The process of returning to a previously explored node in a search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm does not implement backtracking.

glossary.definition: Nodes connected to a given node in the state space graph that have not yet been explored.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it begins at the search start node and pursues a path until (Luger, 2009) : it reaches the search goal node, then it stops and returns the solution path; it reaches a leaf node that is not the search goal node, then it backtracks to the most recent node on the current path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and continues searching; it exhausts the state space graph but does not find the search goal node.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that use only the state space graph or, in other words, its nodes and arcs.
glossary.sentence: In general, there are two main categories of search algorithms (Figure 1): Uninformed search algorithms use only the state space graph or, in other words, its nodes and arcs. This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: A type of uninformed search that explores the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: A type of uninformed search that explores the state space graph sequentially.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that use a heuristic evaluation function to guide the search towards promising states.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform.

glossary.definition: a type of search that processes only nodes and arcs of the state space graph without additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: a type of search that can distinguish between paths in a state space graph by taking into account factors such as distance, location relative to Paris, road surface, number of traffic lights.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others. This is because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement (north, west, etc.) relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that are candidates for expansion during the Beam Search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have already been expanded during the Beam Search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is not the search goal node; thus, the algorithm starts the next iteration.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores the state space graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.
glossary.sentence: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.

glossary.definition: a data structure where elements are added and removed according to the Last in, First Out (LIFO) principle.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: Paths in a state space graph that continue infinitely without reaching a goal node.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores the state space graph up to a predefined depth limit.
glossary.sentence: However, one of the main problems of Depth-Limited Search is a lack of guidelines on how to choose the depth limit for a particular problem.

glossary.definition: A predefined maximum depth to which the search algorithm is allowed to explore in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the maximum depth of the search in Depth-Limited Search, depending on the direction of the search.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: Labels associated with nodes in Depth-Limited Search indicating their depth level in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of the algorithm, the depth limit should be defined, and nodes should be added to both lists together with their depth labels.

glossary.definition: The node being currently processed by the Beam Search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.
glossary.sentence: Breadth-First Search explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: A data structure that follows the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) principle. The first element added to the queue is the first one to be removed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A principle where the first element added to a data structure is the first one to be removed. Queues operate according to this principle.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.

glossary.definition: lists used in Bidirectional Search that are filled in simultaneously in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: lists used in Bidirectional Search that are filled in simultaneously in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: domain-specific knowledge that allows someone to solve problems in a more effective way
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: Used by both N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening to evaluate nodes in the state space graph. It provides a measure of how good a particular state is.
glossary.sentence: Both strategies described above use a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: Numerical values calculated by the heuristic evaluation function and assigned to nodes in a state space graph. They represent the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is incorporated in the heuristic evaluation function, which calculates numerical values called heuristic measures . Heuristic measures are assigned to nodes of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A formula representing the heuristic evaluation function, where n is a node under consideration.
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n. It helps the algorithm choose shorter paths.
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n. It is an estimation of the distance between node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node; this value allows the algorithm to choose promising paths in a state space graph.

glossary.definition: The average number of possible actions or transitions from each state in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node. Usually, we acquire this factor by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes with the total number of nodes in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path from the initial state to a goal state in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A problem where a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead may not detect a promising path, leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A hierarchical representation of all possible moves in a game, starting from an initial state and branching out to all subsequent states.
glossary.sentence: At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the node with the best heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that always chooses the node with the highest (or lowest, depending on the function) heuristic measure compared to the current node.
glossary.sentence: Thus, if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the highest measure, then Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm gets stuck at a node that has a better heuristic measure than its neighbors but is not the goal node.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue because all neighboring nodes have the same heuristic measure as the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that prioritizes exploring nodes with the best heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: Although this algorithm may lead to the search goal node faster than Best-First Search, there are not any guidelines on how the beam width should be chosen.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list in Best-First Search, prioritizing nodes with better heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that combines aspects of Hill Climbing and Best-First Search by expanding a limited number of best nodes in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider an algorithm providing a kind of compromise between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search. Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: A parameter in Beam Search that determines the maximum number of nodes considered at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to a node in the game tree that estimates the potential outcome for a player from that state. It can be precise or intermediate.
glossary.sentence: It assigns a precise heuristic measure of “1” to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A graph that represents all possible states of a game, including the initial state and all subsequent states reachable through valid moves.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A representation of a game where not all possible future states are explicitly represented.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: The player who aims to maximize their score or outcome in a game.
glossary.sentence: The task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: The player who aims to minimize their score or outcome in a game.
glossary.sentence: since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: Nodes in a state space graph that represent terminal states or goal states.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: Nodes in a partial state space graph that represent the end of exploration at a specific depth.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a partial state space graph, a search-based game algorithm is applied to only a sub-graph of the complete search state space graph. As a result, the root and leaf nodes are mostly unavailable. Instead of these nodes, the algorithm processes (Figure 3): a node that represents the current state of the game; and the terminal nodes of the sub-graph at the specified depth, even though it is not possible to identify a game result at any of the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned to the root node of a complete state space graph, indicating the outcome of the game if both players play rationally.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result .

glossary.definition: A sequence of nodes in a complete state space graph that leads from the root node to a leaf node representing a win for a particular player.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken.

glossary.definition: A decision-making algorithm used in game theory to find the optimal move for a player, assuming the opponent also plays optimally.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9). These are the same as for the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm optimization technique that eliminates branches in a game tree that are guaranteed not to affect the final outcome.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9). These are the same as for the minimax algorithm. A more interesting issue is the efficiency of alpha-beta pruning. We can see that two nodes were excluded from the evaluation and several branches were cut off (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A numerical value used in alpha-beta pruning that represents the best move a maximizing player can make up to a given node. It should not decrease as it would represent a worse move.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move. Hence, this number defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game; A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels.

glossary.definition: A numerical value used in alpha-beta pruning that represents the best move a minimizing player can make up to a given node. It should not increase as it would represent a worse move.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.

glossary.definition: Cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: Cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A definitive heuristic measure assigned based on known descendant measures or game results.
glossary.sentence: Step 15: After making the cut off, the node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure assigned when a precise heuristic measure cannot be assigned. It indicates a range of possible measures.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the maximiser makes a move.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the minimiser makes a move.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of <=−1, since the node is located at the MIN level and the only known heuristic measure is −1.

glossary.definition: A branch in the game tree that has not yet been explored by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node.

glossary.definition: A node in the game tree that is located higher up in the hierarchy than another given node. It is connected by a path of parent nodes.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A node in the game tree that is located lower down in the hierarchy than another given node. It is connected by a path of child nodes.
glossary.sentence: When a precise heuristic measure has been assigned, the algorithm goes up one level, along the same path that was used to go down. It then tries to propagate this heuristic measure to other nodes.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The measure of the ancestor node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: Explores a limited number of levels denoted by n, going down from the current state to terminal nodes at a predefined depth. It applies a heuristic evaluation function and propagates the resulting measures back up to the current node using the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: N-ply look-ahead explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.

glossary.definition: Explores the partial state space graph iteratively, going one level deeper at each iteration. It starts with a depth limit of 1 and evaluates nodes using a heuristic evaluation function. The algorithm checks for time limits and can abort the current search at any time.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.

glossary.definition: A type of learning based on labelled data where the goal is to infer a mathematical function describing the relationship between input data and output. It is used for tasks like classification and regression.
glossary.sentence: This type of learning is based on labelled data (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning where the algorithm learns from unlabeled data.
glossary.sentence: Topic 4.3 - Unsupervised machine learning algorithms In the first topic of Module 4, you learnt that unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: A reward-based learning method suitable for agents operating in a changing environment. The agent learns the best sequence of actions to maximize its end goal by receiving feedback in the form of rewards for correct actions and penalties for incorrect ones.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!"

glossary.definition: An algorithm explicitly programmed by developers with a precise set of instructions for each step. It can only provide an output for known inputs.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers.

glossary.definition: a single instance in a dataset, represented by a set of feature values.
glossary.sentence: It is also used in unsupervised machine learning. Therefore, it is worth reminding that the concept of similarity depends on the feature types: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects; for continuous feature values, the geometric distance between any pair of data objects is calculated based on the Euclidean or Manhattan distance: the closer the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: A collection of data objects, each characterized by one or more features and an associated output value. Used to train machine learning models like linear regression.
glossary.sentence: There is a dataset with data collected in the past. The dataset contains the known values of one input variable x , representing a specific feature, i.e. a data object is characterised by one feature (Table 1).

glossary.definition: A specific characteristic or attribute of a data object used as input for a machine learning algorithm like linear regression.
glossary.sentence: Input variables can represent values of different house properties like the house area, the number of floors, land area under the house, etc. Linear regression in this case would provide a prediction of the house price.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a parameter that is set before training a machine learning model and is not learned from the data.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, from the steps given above, one can conclude that this algorithm does not have a separate training phase. It uses all data contained in the dataset for making predictions. It also does not have some assumptions about data distribution. On the other hand, the algorithm is an impractical choice with a large number of data objects because, in this case, the algorithm becomes computationally expensive and time-consuming as it calculates the distance between all the training data objects and a new one data object.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;

glossary.definition: A task in supervised learning where the goal is to predict a continuous value for a given input.
glossary.sentence: Tasks Classification Regression Clustering Association analysis Dimensionality reduction

glossary.definition: a process of dividing an available dataset into subsets (clusters) that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering . It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: A task in unsupervised learning that finds rules based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A task in unsupervised learning associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, often by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019), for example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car's wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: A relationship between two variables where one variable (the independent variable) influences or causes a change in another variable (the dependent variable).
glossary.sentence: For example, we can assume that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the education level of an employee (an independent variable) and his/her salary (a dependent variable), i.e., we assume that the salary depends on the education level: the higher is the education level, the higher is the salary.

glossary.definition: A variable that is believed to influence or cause a change in another variable (the dependent variable).
glossary.sentence: For example, we can assume that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the education level of an employee (an independent variable) and his/her salary (a dependent variable), i.e., we assume that the salary depends on the education level: the higher is the education level, the higher is the salary.

glossary.definition: A variable that is believed to be influenced or caused by another variable (the independent variable).
glossary.sentence: For example, we can assume that there is a cause-and-effect relationship between the education level of an employee (an independent variable) and his/her salary (a dependent variable), i.e., we assume that the salary depends on the education level: the higher is the education level, the higher is the salary.

glossary.definition: Describes a relationship between two variables that can be represented by a straight line in a two-dimensional space.
glossary.sentence: “Linear” means that the relationship expected between the two mentioned variables has a linear pattern and thus can be represented by a straight line in the two-dimensional space, as shown in Figure 1.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and the single output variable (a prediction). Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function.
glossary.sentence: Summary of the main characteristics of the linear regression: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and the single output variable (a prediction). Thus, it uses the linear function. From the given training dataset, it learns the values of weights used in the linear function. The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: Feature values of data objects used in linear regression to predict the output variable.
glossary.sentence: Input variables are feature values of data objects. The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: A prediction made by the linear regression model, represented as a real number. It is the value being predicted based on the input variables.
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model. It is a real number in a range from minus infinity to infinity.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function that represents a straight line in a two-dimensional space. It is used in linear regression to model the relationship between input and output variables.
glossary.sentence: In mathematics, the linear relationship between two variables x and y is represented by the following function: y=m*x +b, where x – the value of the input variable, y – the value of the output variable, m – a real number that corresponds to the slope of the line, b – a real number that corresponds to the intercept (a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0).

glossary.definition: A measure of the steepness of a line represented by the coefficient 'm' in the linear function. It indicates how much the output variable changes for every unit change in the input variable.
glossary.sentence: where x – the value of the input variable, y – the value of the output variable, m – a real number that corresponds to the slope of the line, b – a real number that corresponds to the intercept (a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0).

glossary.definition: The point where a line crosses the y-axis. It is represented by the coefficient 'b' in the linear function and indicates the value of the output variable when the input variable is zero.
glossary.sentence: b – a real number that corresponds to the intercept (a point where the line crosses the y axis or, in other words, it is the value of y when x is equal to 0).

glossary.definition: Another name for the intercept 'b' in the linear function. It represents the value of the output variable when the input variable is zero.
glossary.sentence: It is also usually called a bias term/coefficient .

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression that uses only one input feature to predict the output variable.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, there are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression that uses multiple input features to predict the output variable.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, there are two types of linear regression (Russell and Norvig, 2010): univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model

glossary.definition: In the context of linear regression with multiple input features, it represents the geometric representation of the relationship between the input features and the output variable. It extends the concept of a line to higher dimensions.
