The equation of the linear regression in more generalised form looks like this: We can re-write the equation given above using matrices and vectors: In the case of multiple input features, the linear relationship between input features and the output value is represented not like a straight line but as a plane or a hyperplane (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A generalization of a plane to higher dimensions. In linear regression with multiple input features, it represents the relationship between the input features and the output variable in a space with more than three dimensions.
glossary.sentence: The fragment of the dataset is given in Table 4. The equation of the linear regression in more generalised form looks like this: We can re-write the equation given above using matrices and vectors: In the case of multiple input features, the linear relationship between input features and the output value is represented not like a straight line but as a plane or a hyperplane (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: The process of finding the values for the weights in the linear regression equation that minimize the error between the predicted output values and the actual output values in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, training a linear regression model means finding the values of the weights that minimise the empirical loss (error) across all data objects available in the dataset (Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: The difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value. It measures how well the model fits the data.
glossary.sentence: The difference between the output value predicted by the linear regression model and the real output value is called an error . Therefore, the linear regression tries to learn such weights of the linear function that minimise the total error across the whole dataset.

glossary.term: squared loss (also known as L2 loss)
glossary.definition: The loss function used in linear regression to measure the difference between predicted and actual values. It squares the difference between each prediction and its corresponding actual value.
glossary.sentence: The fragment of the dataset is given in Table 4. The equation of the linear regression in more generalised form looks like this: We can re-write the equation given above using matrices and vectors: In the case of multiple input features, the linear relationship between input features and the output value is represented not like a straight line but as a plane or a hyperplane (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: Values that modify input values at each layer of a network.
glossary.sentence: To answer this question, it is necessary to consider that input values are modified by the weights at each layer of the network.

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.

glossary.definition: A positive constant between 0 and 1 that determines how quickly weights are adjusted during learning.
glossary.sentence: It is a positive constant which is a small real number between 0 and 1. The learning rate determines how quickly the weights will change in the process of learning.

glossary.definition: A supervised machine learning task where the goal is to predict the class or category to which a data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs. Since the classification is a task of supervised machine learning, then actual class labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels.

glossary.definition: A type of classification task with only two possible classes or categories, represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive );

glossary.definition: A type of classification task with more than two classes to which data objects can belong. It is an extension of binary classification.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging. The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.

glossary.definition: It is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to, for example, success/failure, good/bad, yes/no, win/loss, etc.

glossary.definition: Logistic regression also tries finding a mathematical function that relates x to y . It also uses the linear function as the linear regression does. Still, after receiving the value of the linear function, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also tries finding a mathematical function that relates x to y . It also uses the linear function as the linear regression does. Still, after receiving the value of the linear function, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks. Let’s define the idea of this algorithm based on the classification task.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks. Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task. K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: a method used to determine the similarity of data objects, such as distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: data objects that are closest to a new data object based on a similarity metric.
glossary.sentence: The nearest neighbours are identified for two new data objects based on distance and one data object receives a label of sun, another – of cloud.

glossary.definition: descriptions of data objects based on their features (attributes).
glossary.sentence: In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: a measure of how similar two data objects are based on their features.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: numerical feature values that can take on any value within a given range.
glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects: the closer to each other the data objects, the greater their mutual similarity.

glossary.definition: a geometric distance between any pair of data objects used for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset

glossary.definition: a geometric distance between any pair of data objects used for continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset

glossary.definition: feature values that are represented as discrete categories or classes.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: counts the number of features in which two data objects differ; used for categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: for categorical feature values, usually, the Hamming distance is used that counts the number of features in which two data objects differ: the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects

glossary.definition: datasets that include both continuous and categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017):

glossary.definition: a version of the kNN algorithm where only the single nearest neighbor is used to classify a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: a version of the kNN algorithm where k nearest neighbors are used to classify a new data object.
glossary.sentence: However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries. Figure 11 shows an example of 1NN algorithm when two data objects are classified based on the nearest neighbour. In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used.

glossary.definition: a parameter that defines the number of nearest neighbors to consider when classifying a new data object in the kNN algorithm. Determined by the developer before running the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: a method used in the kNN algorithm where the class label of a new data object is determined by the majority vote of its k nearest neighbors.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: a machine learning algorithm that calculates the distance between a new data object and data objects from the dataset; sorts them by distance; picks the top k data objects; and returns the mode (classification) or mean (regression) of their labels.
glossary.sentence: 17): calculate the distance (based on Euclidean, Manhattan or Hamming distance) between a new data object whose output value should be determined and the current data object from the dataset; store the index of the data objects and the calculated distances in a list; sort the list of data objects and distances in ascending order from the smallest distance to the largest one; pick up the top k data objects from the sorted list; get the labels of the selected data objects; identify the output for the new data object: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class); in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: the number of clusters specified for the K-Means algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Specify the number of clusters, K , that need to be generated by this algorithm. Thus, K is the hyperparameter of this algorithm. Usually, it is selected by a trial-and-error approach.

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning task where the algorithm predicts a continuous value.
glossary.sentence: in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: the most frequent class in a set of labels.
glossary.sentence: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class)

glossary.definition: the average value of a set of numbers.
glossary.sentence: in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.

glossary.definition: a process of scaling feature values to a common range, often required by machine learning algorithms like kNN.
glossary.sentence: kNN algorithm typically demands the normalization of feature value. Two common methods for normalisation are the following: On the one hand,

glossary.definition: a subset of data objects that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.).

glossary.definition: a representation of a data object as a set of feature values.
glossary.sentence: It is a process of dividing an available dataset (data objects represented by feature vectors) into subsets that share some similarities.

glossary.definition: a measure of how alike two data objects are.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each cluster is made of one or more data objects and characterised by two aspects: the similarity of data objects within the cluster; the difference of data objects between the clusters. The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm.

glossary.definition: the centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” . The algorithm is based on two central concepts: the concept of distance; the concept of a centroid. A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above). A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009). It means that the data objects that are close to the centroid in terms of distance make a cluster.

glossary.definition: an unsupervised machine learning algorithm that groups data objects into clusters by minimizing the sum of distances between data objects and their assigned centroids. It requires specifying the number of clusters (K) beforehand.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” . The algorithm is based on two central concepts: the concept of distance; the concept of a centroid. A distance represents a similarity measure used to group data objects in clusters (see above). A centroid is a centre of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid. It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009). It means that the data objects that are close to the centroid in terms of distance make a cluster.

glossary.definition: A bottom-up clustering approach that starts with each data object as a separate cluster and repeatedly merges the two closest clusters until all data objects belong to a single cluster.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level. Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with all data objects in a single cluster and recursively splits it into smaller clusters until each data object forms its own cluster.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: A measure of how different two data objects or clusters are. Lower dissimilarity indicates greater similarity.
glossary.sentence: take the two data objects with the smallest dissimilarity and make them one cluster.

glossary.definition: Methods used to determine the distance between clusters based on the distances between their individual data objects. Different linkage methods lead to different clustering results.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the maximum distance between any pair of data objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the minimum distance between any pair of data objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the average distance between all pairs of data objects from the two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A tree-like diagram that visualizes the hierarchical relationships between data objects in a dataset. It shows how clusters are formed and merged at different levels of similarity.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” .

glossary.definition: In a dendrogram, a branch that represents the merging of data objects into a cluster. The height of a clade indicates the similarity between the merged clusters.
glossary.sentence: clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: In a dendrogram, the terminal points representing individual data objects. They are connected by clades, showing their hierarchical relationships.
glossary.sentence: clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: A horizontal line drawn across a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters. The number of vertical lines intersected by the cut-off line represents the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: A metric calculated by squaring the difference between predicted output values and actual output values, resulting in units that are squared units of the target variable. Smaller MSE values indicate better model predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: It is calculated in the following way: This metric has the same units of measurement as the quantity being estimated. Still, they are squared units; for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, the error will represent "the squared number of persons"; if it predicts the house prices in EUR, then the error value will represent "squared EUR". This type of error is prone to outliers.

glossary.definition: Applies a square root to MSE, providing an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. Smaller RMSE values indicate better model predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable, for example, if the classifier predicts the number of graduates, then the error will represent “the number of persons”; if it predicts the prices of the house in EUR, then the error value will represent “price in EUR”. The smaller the value, the better the predictive accuracy of the model is.

glossary.definition: The average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. It is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination, it indicates how well a model fits the data. Values range from 0 to 1, where 0 means no fit and 1 means perfect fit. It represents the proportion of variance in actual output values explained by the model's features.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset. The coefficient values lie in a range [0,1], where 0 indicates that the model does not fit the data objects and 1 indicates the perfect fit. It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features. The coefficent is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: Correct predictions where a data object is positive (label 1) and the algorithm correctly recognizes it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: Correct predictions where a data object is negative (label 0) and the algorithm correctly recognizes it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions where a data object is negative (label 0) but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1).
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: Erroneous predictions where a data object is positive (label 1) but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0).
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table showing the counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category. It provides detailed information on correct and erroneous predictions for each class.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017) . It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010) . Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Evaluates the fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017) :

glossary.definition: Evaluates the fraction of predicted positives that are true positives, representing the accuracy of positive predictions. It is calculated as true positives divided by the sum of true positives and false positives. A higher value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions (Geron, 2017) . Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017) . It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of precision lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.

glossary.definition: Evaluates the fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. It is also known as sensitivity and true positive rate. It represents the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognized by the algorithm. Calculated as true positives divided by the sum of true positives and false negatives. A higher value indicates better predictive accuracy.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. According to (Kubat, 2017) , recall is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017; Geron, 2017) : The value of recall lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy.

glossary.definition: The inherent conflict between precision and recall, where increasing one often reduces the other. Achieving high precision usually means sacrificing some recall, and vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff .

glossary.definition: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced metric for evaluating classifier performance. It represents the number of data objects classified correctly. Calculated as 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall). A high F1 score indicates both high precision and recall.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high (Geron, 2017) . Thus, it estimates the number of data objects classified correctly. It is calculated in the following way (Chapelle et al., 2006; Geron, 2017) : The value of the F1 score lies in the range [0,1]. A high F-score indicates that the precision and recall are high and close to each other (Chapelle et al., 2006) . Thus, the better the model performance is.

glossary.definition: Receiver Operating Characteristic curve. It plots the false positive rate (x-axis) against the true positive rate (y-axis) for different values of a classifier's hyperparameter. It helps visualize the tradeoff between these rates and identify good hyperparameter choices.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve (Kubat, 2017) . The ROC curve plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping you visualize values that would be good choices for the tradeoff (Russell & Norvig, 2010; Geron, 2017; Kubat, 2017) .

glossary.definition: Area Under the ROC Curve. A single-number summary of the ROC curve, measuring the entire two-dimensional area underneath it. It ranges from 0 to 1. A value of 1 indicates a perfect classifier, while 0.5 represents random chance.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve (Russell & Norvig, 2010) . It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve. It returns a value in the range [0, 1]. A highly accurate classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 1; in contrast, a purely random classifier will have a ROC AUC equal to 0.5 (Geron, 2017) .

glossary.definition: A metric for evaluating clustering performance that does not require ground-truth labels. It measures the similarity of each data object to its own cluster compared to other clusters. Calculated as (b - a) / max(a, b), where 'a' is the average intra-cluster distance and 'b' is the average inter-cluster distance. Values range from -1 to 1, with higher values indicating better separation of clusters.
glossary.sentence: One of the metrics that do not demand ground-truth labels is the Silhouette Score . The Silhouette Score is calculated by the following way: where a - average intra-cluster distance (the average distance between each point within a cluster), b - average inter-cluster distance (the average distance between all clusters). Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. The values of the Silhouette score lies in a range [-1, 1]. The closer values of the Silhouette score is to +1, the better is clusters are separated. A value of 0 indicates that the distance between neighbouring clusters is not significant. Hence, data objects lie on the decision boundary. Negative values of the Silhouette score indicate that data objects might have been assigned to the wrong cluster.

glossary.definition: A visualization of the Silhouette Score for each data object in a clustering solution. It plots cluster labels on the y-axis and Silhouette Scores on the x-axis, with the size of the silhouettes proportional to the number of data objects within each cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6). The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: A concept closely related to artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Nowadays, artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning task that can be carried out by artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks. This topic will look mainly at artificial neural networks, which can be used for the classification task of supervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning task that can be carried out by artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks. This topic will look mainly at artificial neural networks, which can be used for the classification task of supervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain that consists of a soma (cell body) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions: dendrites and an axon. Dendrites receive signals from other neurons, while the axon transmits signals to other neurons via synapses.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: The cell body of a neuron, containing the nucleus.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions of a neuron that resemble a brush and receive signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A single root-like extension of a neuron that transmits signals to other neurons via synapses.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. Signals are transmitted across synapses.
glossary.sentence: When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron.

glossary.definition: A synapse that promotes firing of a neuron. It is associated with a positive weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A synapse that inhibits firing of a neuron. It is associated with a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A numerical value that expresses the strength or importance of a synapse in influencing the output of a neuron. Excitatory synapses have positive weights, while inhibitory synapses have negative weights.
glossary.sentence: If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron.

glossary.definition: A value that, when exceeded by the total input to a neuron, causes the neuron to fire.
glossary.sentence: If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)).

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of a biological neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943. It has inputs, weights, an output, a combination function, a transfer (activation) function, and optionally a bias input.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Numerical values that enter an artificial neuron from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. They represent data object features in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons.

glossary.definition: A real-valued number associated with each input of an artificial neuron. It expresses the strength or importance of the input in influencing the output. Weights are the means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks. They are determined in the process of neural network learning.

glossary.definition: A numerical value produced by an artificial neuron. It can be a final solution or input to other neurons.
glossary.sentence: an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons.

glossary.definition: A function used by an artificial neuron to combine its weighted inputs. Typically, it is a summation function, but other functions are also allowed.
glossary.sentence: a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value. It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs. However, other functions are also allowed;

glossary.definition: A function used by an artificial neuron to determine its output based on the combined input value. It allows the network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships;

glossary.definition: A constant input added to each processing unit to adjust the activation threshold.
glossary.sentence: Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function. According to ( Negnevitsky , 2011), commercial artificial neural networks incorporate three-four layers but experimental ones - five-six layers, while most practical applications use only three layers because each next layer increases computational complexity exponentially.

glossary.definition: also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern (Callan, 2003; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. They are connected by transmitting signals to each other.

glossary.definition: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.
glossary.sentence: Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: The layer of a neural network that receives input signals from the environment.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an input layer that accepts input signals from the environment and sends these signals to hidden layers. It does not include computing neurons;

glossary.definition: The layer of a neural network that produces the final output.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): an output layer that sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment and consists of computing neurons;

glossary.definition: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers. The output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers . The output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: neurons that means that this output becomes an input for other neurons. Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.
glossary.sentence: Most neurons in artificial neural networks send this output to other neurons that means that this output becomes an input for other neurons. Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). This parallelism allows artificial neural networks to take advantage of parallel processing computers (Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009). This parallelism allows artificial neural networks to take advantage of parallel processing computers (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input.
glossary.sentence: Based on the way of connecting neurons, two types of artificial neural networks can be distinguished (Russell & Norvig, 2010): A feed-forward network has connections only in one dir • ection . Every processing unit receives input from "upstream" processing units and delivers output to "downstream" processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input.

glossary.definition: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour. Moreover, the network's response to a given input depends on its initial state, which may depend on previous inputs.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network , on the other hand, feeds its outputs back into its inputs. It means that the activation levels of the network form a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or even chaotic behaviour. Moreover, the network's response to a given input depends on its initial state, which may depend on previous inputs.

glossary.definition: It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way . It means that after processing the input signals in the whole network, the artificial neural network receives a final output which in the case of supervised machine learning can be compared with the desired (actual label) output. In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).
glossary.sentence: It is called fault tolerance, or, in other words, graceful degradation, and it is explained by the fact that the knowledge of the network is not stored in one place, but it is distributed all over the network ( Borisovs et al., 1998).

glossary.definition: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.definition: projecting probable consequences of given situations
glossary.sentence: Prediction - projecting probable consequences of given situations.

glossary.definition: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" input;
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks (Jones, 2009; Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009): optimization or, in other words, finding the "best" input;

glossary.definition: A Single Layer Perceptron (SLP) is a type of artificial neural network that consists of a single layer of processing units. Each unit receives input from all the inputs, performs a weighted sum of these inputs, and then applies an activation function to produce an output.
A type of artificial neural network that learns the best possible values for weights to correctly classify data.
glossary.sentence: The perceptron learns the best possible values for weights that will allow correctly to classify the data (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A data object used to teach a perceptron to classify.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: The correct classification label for a training example.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output) is given to the perceptron;

glossary.definition: The classification label produced by the perceptron for a given training example.
glossary.sentence: after receiving the output of the perceptron, the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output;

glossary.definition: A mathematical function used by a perceptron to process input signals.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit;

glossary.definition: A value used by a perceptron to determine if its output should be activated.
glossary.sentence: the initial weights are randomly selected, and the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5 ];

glossary.definition: One cycle in which an artificial neural network uses all data objects in the training dataset for learning.
glossary.sentence: An epoch represents one cycle in which the artificial neural network uses all of the data objects contained in the training dataset exactly once for learning purposes.

glossary.definition: The sum of squared differences between actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.
glossary.sentence: The total error of the network is calculated as the sum of squared differences between the actual and predicted outputs for all output neurons for all training examples.

glossary.definition: A type of feed-forward neural network that can represent continuous and discontinuous functions.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network that, with one hidden layer, can represent any continuous function of the input signals and with two hidden layers - even discontinuous functions ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A type of neural network where information flows in one direction from input to output.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a feed-forward neural network has greater processing power, and it can model practically any function of arbitrary complexity, where the number of inputs and the number of hidden layers determine the function complexity (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A layer of a neural network between the input and output layers that performs feature detection.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features. Neurons in the hidden layer cannot be observed through the input/output behaviour, and there is no obvious way to know what the desired output of the hidden layer should be.

glossary.definition: A neuron in a neural network that performs calculations.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer. However, neurons at the same layer are not connected.

glossary.definition: An algorithm used to train multilayer perceptrons by propagating error backwards through the network.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: The process of propagating inputs forward through a neural network.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: The difference between the predicted output and the real class label.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the weights of all layers contribute to the total error. Therefore, to reduce the error at the output, the responsibility for the error should be shared among all layers.

glossary.definition: Values that are modified by the weights at each layer of a network during propagation.
glossary.sentence: To answer this question, it is necessary to consider that input values are modified by the weights at each layer of the network.

glossary.definition: A structural component of a network where input values are modified by weights.
glossary.sentence: To answer this question, it is necessary to consider that input values are modified by the weights at each layer of the network.

glossary.definition: Units within a network responsible for a fraction of the error in each output unit.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing units is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: Units in a network that produce the final result.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that modifies weights in all network layers by propagating error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: A collection of production rules that store knowledge in a rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: The component of a rule-based expert system responsible for processing knowledge and making inferences.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine.

glossary.definition: A collection of unrelated facts.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: methods used by intelligent computer systems, divided into two groups: weak methods and strong methods
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: the process of getting knowledge into a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base? There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001)

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: anyone considered such if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain
glossary.sentence: However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert. Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an automated system used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and build a knowledge base
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance in solving problems in a narrowly specialized domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains)
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge
glossary.sentence: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/ concept;

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions
glossary.sentence: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992). Therefore, a knowledge base rooted in semantic networks includes a network of concepts and their relations. Graphically semantic networks are represented using only two symbols (Fig.5).

glossary.definition: A relation represented by titles such as “is a”, “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”. It shows hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”). It allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A group of concepts or objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “is an instance/example of”. It allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to.
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”. It also allows creating hierarchical relations by relating one specific object in the problem domain to a class/set/type/kind it belongs to (Fig.7).

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “Part of”. It allows relating parts of an object to the whole object.
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of” (Fig.8). It allows relating parts of the object to the whole object.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the titles “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties.
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”. It allows relating objects/concepts to their properties (Fig.9).

glossary.definition: A characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values. This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”. It allows relating properties of objects/concepts to their values (Fig.10). This relation can come only after the relation “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: Relations that allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: A mechanism in frames where subclasses inherit properties and slots from their parent classes. This allows for efficient knowledge reuse and organization.
glossary.sentence: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results;

glossary.definition: a data structure for representing a stereotyped situation; a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities
glossary.sentence: A frame is “a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation” (Minsky, 1975). In other words, it is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky , 2011) or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: properties of objects/concepts; each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Each slot has a unique identifier in the knowledge base. Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts; typically includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind (Figure 4). Traditionally slot values in the class frame are empty, or they contain default values.
glossary.sentence: There are two main types of frames (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain; typically include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames. However, instance frames can also contain slots that are not defined for class/sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: used to relate two frames; means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7). Therefore, frames are related only by slot values, not by slot titles , because slot titles explain what kind of slot values is stored in the frame.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relationship between frames where a subclass or instance is connected to its parent class. This reflects the inheritance structure of knowledge within the system.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation is defined between TreeAlex-Person , BrownLucie-Person , Tom-Cat , Rex-Dog , Cat-Animal , Dog-Animal .

glossary.definition: allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole . In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by slot titles “a-part-of” or “part-whole”.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .

glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.sentence: Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values. glossary.definition: can be added to a slot. A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc. Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed (Mallach, 1994). Thus, demons control actions for slot-level manipulations (Jones, 2009). Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames. Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: a standard demon triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles. The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: a standard demon triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles. The common demons are: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: a standard demon triggered when a new slot is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles. The common demons are: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a new slot is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: Executable code parts within frames that are triggered automatically. They define actions to be taken when specific conditions are met.
glossary.sentence: Although the demons are triggered automatically, their executable part (the code describing what will happen when the demon is called) should be developed by the developer of the intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation structure that stores information about objects or concepts in a structured way. They allow for hierarchical organization and inheritance of knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Figure 13 represents a frame-based knowledge base that stores knowledge about persons and their home animals like cats and dogs.

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when a new instance (frame) is added to the knowledge base. It performs calculations or actions related to the newly added instance.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-ADDED will calculate the value of the slot Ensured services when a new client (a frame instance) would be added to the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when specific information or knowledge is requested. It calculates or retrieves the required information.
glossary.sentence: The demon IF-NEEDED will calculate the amount of discount for a particular client when the information on the discount will be requested.

glossary.definition: Named attributes or properties within a frame that store specific information about the object or concept represented by the frame. They can hold values, demons, or other data.
glossary.sentence: If necessary knowledge is available in the object frame, return it; if necessary knowledge is not found, then the inference engine can apply the steps specified below and stop when the necessary knowledge is found: perform inheritance (if it is possible ); check inherited demons IF-NEEDED. If they are, trigger them and output the results; explore the local relations with other frames and try to get knowledge from them.

glossary.definition: Frames that represent general concepts or categories. They define common properties and slots that are inherited by subclass frames.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class.

glossary.definition: Frames that represent specific instances or types within a broader category defined by a class frame. They inherit properties from their parent class frame and can have additional unique slots.
glossary.sentence: Next, there are two sub-class frames: Cat and Dog . They inherit slots from the Animal class frame, and each of them has new slots added: Affectionate in the Cat sub-class and Roar and Barking in the Dog sub-class.

glossary.definition: Frames that represent individual objects or specific cases within a class or subclass. They hold concrete data values for the slots defined by their parent frames.
glossary.sentence: Finally, there are four instance frames: TreeAlex and BrownLucie are instances for the Person class frame; Tom is an instance for the Cat class frame; Rex is an instance for the Dog class frame.

glossary.definition: Links or connections between different frames that represent specific relationships between objects or concepts. Examples include Owns, Animal owner, Afraid of.
glossary.sentence: The frame system also represents the following associative relations between frames: BrownLucie - Tom , the relation Owns , and Tom- BrownLucie , the relation Animal owner; TreeAlex - Rex , the relation Owns , and Rex- TreeAlex , the relation Animal owner; Tom – Rex , the relation Afraid of .

glossary.definition: Statements written in an IF-THEN format that represent knowledge in a rule-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems that consist of three main components: a knowledge base containing production rules, a working memory to store facts and conclusions, and an inference engine to apply the rules.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A temporary storage area where facts and data relevant to the current problem-solving session are held.
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory.

glossary.definition: a description of the current state of the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: are separated from the knowledge base and are assumed to be correct when they are used.
glossary.sentence: These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result.

glossary.definition: allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: implemented by the control cycle "Recognize-act" to find a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle “Recognize-act” makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules.

glossary.definition: The set of rules that can be fired at a given time.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.
glossary.sentence: Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.

glossary.definition: The method used to choose which rule to fire when multiple rules can be triggered simultaneously.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: used to select a rule from the enabled rules.
glossary.sentence: For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: used to make the search more effective during the matching of production rules against the pattern. There are three types: control through the selection of search direction; control through the selection of rule sequence and structure; control through the selection of conflict resolution strategy.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to perform two types of search in rule-based expert systems (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): data-driven search (forward chaining ); goal-driven search (backward chaining). In both cases, the search starts from the first rule in the knowledge base, and only a way how the rules are explored differs.

glossary.definition: A value associated with a production rule that represents the degree of certainty or confidence in the rule.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.
